sections.text: Welcome to English One, Module Five — Narrative Writing. A real story is not a list of events. It is a shape — five stages of a plot, one point of view, and three techniques that make a reader feel they were there. Today you build all three so your personal narrative reads like a scene, not a summary.

Here is the trap. A student writes — I went to camp. It was fun. I learned a lot. Three sentences, zero scene. Now read this — The bus doors hissed open, and the pine smell hit me before my feet touched the gravel. Same trip. Different shape. The first one tells. The second one shows.

Game plan. First, the five-stage plot arc — every story you remember has the same shape. Second, point of view — first person for personal narrative, and why. Third, the three moves of a real scene — dialogue, sensory detail, and pacing. Then we pause twice — once to outline an arc, once to choose between telling and showing.

The arc has five stages. Exposition — set the scene and the stakes. Rising action — the problem grows, the pressure climbs. Climax — one peak moment where everything turns. Falling action — the aftermath unfolds. Resolution — the new normal. A 500-word narrative still hits all five — just smaller and tighter.

Point of view is the camera. First person — I — sees through one character's eyes and feels honest for personal stories. Second person — you — is rare and pushes the reader into the action. Third person — he, she, they — sees from outside, more flexible but less intimate. For your personal narrative, first person is the rule.

The most common mistake — write a diary entry instead of a scene. Diary entry — I woke up. I ate breakfast. We left. Three flat verbs, no shape. Scene — the alarm rang twice before I sat up, and my mother's voice was already in the hallway. Same morning, different camera. Diary entries report. Scenes happen.

Trace a tiny narrative — the day I learned to swim. Exposition — I was seven, terrified of the deep end. Rising action — Coach Rivera made me hold the wall while the water rose to my chin. Climax — my fingers slipped. Falling action — I kicked once, then again, and my head stayed up. Resolution — I let go on purpose. Five stages. Fifty words.

Your turn. On screen — a prompt — the first time I stood on a stage. Outline the five stages in five short sentences — exposition, rising action, climax, falling action, resolution. Use first person. Be concrete — name one detail per stage. Pause now. Outline it. Press play when you are ready.

Time. One possible arc — Exposition — I was twelve, holding a violin that felt too big. Rising action — the hall darkened, and my bow shook on the first note. Climax — I lost the page and froze for two long seconds. Falling action — my teacher mouthed the next bar. Resolution — I finished, breathing hard. Yours will differ — what matters is all five stages and one concrete detail each.

One more. Two ways to write the same moment — Version A — I was scared and my parents were proud. Version B — My mother whispered breathe through the back of my chair, and my father's camera click was the loudest sound in the room. Pick the better narrative move. Pause now. Explain why. Press play when ready.

Time. Version B wins. Same emotion, different camera. Version A tells the reader what to feel — scared, proud — and gives the reader nothing to see. Version B shows the moment — whispered words, a camera click — and the feeling rises from the scene itself. Telling reports. Showing happens. Use dialogue, sensory detail, and small precise action.

Now the assignment payoff. Write a personal narrative of at least 500 words about a meaningful experience. Hit all five plot stages. Stay in first person. Include at least three lines of dialogue. Lean on sensory detail — what you saw, heard, smelled. Close with a one-sentence reflection on what the moment meant. Submit a pre-writing outline with your draft.

Recap. Story arc — exposition, rising action, climax, falling action, resolution. Point of view — first person for personal narrative. Three moves of a real scene — dialogue, sensory detail, pacing. Show, do not tell — the scene carries the feeling, not the label.

How to master this. Read the Purdue OWL pages on the writing process — planning, drafting, revising. Watch Khan Academy on point of view in narrative writing. In your Learn Portal dashboard, complete the 500-word personal narrative with the pre-writing outline. Book an advisor check-in if either pause felt shaky. Next up — Module Six, Argumentative Writing.
